title: Why American data centers can't plug in
gist: Works in Progress explains that the binding constraint on the US AI buildout is not power generation but grid interconnection: median wait times have grown from under 20 months in 2005 to 55 months by 2023, ERCOT has 143.5 GW of data centers waiting to connect versus only 85.9 GW peak demand, and the first-come, first-served queue creates a tragedy of the commons—the proposed solution is auction-based prioritization and energy-only 'connect and manage' service.

insights.claim: The primary bottleneck to AI data center growth is not power generation or energy scarcity but the grid interconnection process: connecting new infrastructure requires system impact studies and upgrade construction that has grown from a median wait of under 20 months in 2005 to 55 months by 2023.
insights.speaker: Works in Progress
insights.so_what: Grid interconnection delay is the binding constraint on the AI buildout—not coal, nuclear, or solar availability; companies and investors focused on power generation are solving the wrong problem, while the interconnection queue backlog is the actual bottleneck to be unlocked.
insights.quote: the median power plant in 2005 waited less than 20 months for interconnection, this had jumped to 55 months by 2023
insights.locator: Though the median power plant in 2005

insights.claim: As of October 2025, ERCOT had 143.5 gigawatts of data centers seeking to connect—compared to ERCOT's highest-ever total demand of 85.9 gigawatts recorded in August 2024—illustrating the scale of the connection backlog relative to total grid capacity.
insights.speaker: Works in Progress
insights.so_what: The data center interconnection queue in ERCOT alone is 1.7x total peak grid demand—a structural supply-demand mismatch in grid access that will take years to work through under current rules, creating an enduring bottleneck for AI infrastructure investment in Texas.
insights.quote: there were 143.5 gigawatts of data centers seeking to connect as of October 2025. This compares to ERCOT's highest ever total demand of 85.9 gigawatts in August 2024.

insights.claim: PJM's 2025 capacity auction cost $14.7 billion—up from $2.2 billion the year before—and a study found that if just 30% of queued renewable projects had been interconnected, the auction cost would have been 63% lower, quantifying the direct consumer and industry cost of the interconnection backlog.
insights.speaker: Works in Progress
insights.so_what: The PJM auction surge from $2.2B to $14.7B is a concrete financial cost attributable to the interconnection queue failure; for power-intensive industries (data centers, manufacturing), this escalation in capacity costs is a direct input cost headwind that won't ease until the queue clears.
insights.quote: In 2025, PJM's electricity suppliers paid $14.7 billion at auction to make sure they'd have enough power to supply their customers, a huge jump from $2.2 billion the year before.

insights.claim: Stargate—OpenAI and SoftBank's flagship AI campus in Abilene, Texas—is expected to cost well over $40 billion and draw 1.2 gigawatts at peak load, equivalent to 313,000 median American family homes; this is one example in what Works in Progress calls 'one of the biggest investment booms in history.'
insights.speaker: Works in Progress
insights.so_what: Stargate's 1.2 GW demand profile illustrates why large individual projects are transformative grid events—each Stargate-scale campus requires dedicated transmission infrastructure and creates interconnection challenges that are non-trivial regardless of available generation.
insights.quote: Stargate is expected to cost well over $40 billion for a high-performance computing campus that will train new generations of AI models

insights.claim: xAI's Memphis data center initially received only 8 megawatts from the grid; rather than wait for grid upgrades, xAI installed 422 megawatts of on-site gas turbines. As of October 2025, 62% of data centers are considering off-grid solutions to bypass the interconnection queue.
insights.speaker: Works in Progress
insights.so_what: The xAI precedent signals a market workaround: hyperscalers are self-generating power to escape the grid queue, concentrating gas turbine demand, increasing local emissions, and adding cost vs. grid power—but it is the rational response to a broken interconnection process.
insights.quote: 62 percent of data centers are considering off-grid solutions, either to get up and running faster or to improve reliability
insights.locator: Such off-grid generation is a temporary solution

insights.claim: The first-come, first-served interconnection queue is a 'tragedy of the commons': 72% of requests since 2000 were ultimately withdrawn, speculative and duplicative requests clog the system, and developers rationally spam the queue because capacity is first-come and essentially free.
insights.speaker: Works in Progress
insights.so_what: The queue's failure is structural, not operational: it is incorrectly designed as a commons with no price signal—any reform that doesn't introduce pricing or auctions will continue to generate phantom backlogs regardless of process improvements.
insights.quote: 72 percent of requests to connect submitted since 2000 were ultimately withdrawn...The flood of requests is a typical 'tragedy of the commons'. Everyone is incentivized to spam the queue with requests.

insights.claim: Works in Progress proposes auctioning 'fast-track cluster' slots so the most viable and valuable projects jump the queue: developers' bids would capture private knowledge (permitting risk, project viability) that administrative scoring cannot—analogous to tradable fishing quotas replacing the seasonal dash for fish.
insights.speaker: Works in Progress
insights.so_what: Auction-based interconnection is the key policy lever to unlock US AI grid capacity; investors in grid infrastructure, AI data centers, and power generation should monitor FERC rulemaking for auction-based queue reform as the most important structural change to watch.
insights.quote: Auctioning new grid capacity could bring similar benefits. The scramble for interconnection would be replaced with an orderly process in which the highest quality projects would get priority.
insights.locator: Auctioning new grid capacity could bring

insights.claim: Works in Progress identifies a 'data center trilemma': data centers can be large, come online quickly, or receive firm grid service—but not all three simultaneously. Flexible data centers willing to disconnect for a few hours per year (using on-site batteries) could add 76 GW of new load across most of the US under connect-and-manage rules.
insights.speaker: Works in Progress
insights.so_what: The trilemma framing explains why different AI players are making different infrastructure bets (speed vs. size vs. reliability); flexible, battery-backed data centers willing to operate on non-firm transmission could bypass years of queue waiting—a direct capex and operational strategy implication for hyperscalers.
insights.quote: data centers can be large, they can come online quickly, or they can receive firm grid service, but not all three
insights.locator: Disconnecting from the grid isn't exactly ideal
